description: No startup-shaped test builds the plugin's Guice/HiberniaModule graph and invokes CommandManager.registerAll() / ListenerManager.registerAll(). The charter requires such a test so that duplicate @Command route registrations and missing i18n message keys fail in CI. Nothing under treasury/src/test constructs HiberniaModule or calls registerAll (grep for 'registerAll'/'HiberniaModule'/'CommandManager' in tests returns only unrelated 'route' substring matches). Route conflicts and dangling message keys therefore surface only at server boot, not in the build. The command tests instantiate command objects directly, bypassing framework registration entirely.
recommendation: Add a wiring/startup test that constructs the real HiberniaModule + TreasuryModule injector (with a stub DataSource or the IT MariaDB), resolves CommandManager and ListenerManager, and calls registerAll() — asserting it completes without throwing. Extend it to assert every message key referenced by @Command/help routes exists in messages.properties so key drift fails CI.
verification_note: Snippet 'injector.getInstance(CommandManager.class).registerAll();' matches verbatim at Treasury.java:143, with ListenerManager.registerAll() at :146. The factual claims hold: grep for registerAll/HiberniaModule/CommandManager under treasury/src/test returns nothing; the only 'route' hits are unrelated test-method names (BalanceTaxFallbackIT:59 'routesToDefaultTaxAccount', etc.). IntegrationTestBase.java:39-42 constructs a Guice injector but wires only DatabaseModule + TestServicesModule — never HiberniaModule/TreasuryModule and never calls registerAll(). Command tests (TransactionsCommandTest:51) instantiate commands directly via 'new TransactionsCommand(...)', bypassing framework registration. So the registration path (duplicate @Command routes, missing i18n keys) is genuinely untested and only surfaces at boot. However this is a missing-test/hardening gap, not a live defect: route conflicts fail loudly and immediately at server boot on the next deploy, the dev workflow already includes live boots for runtime-untestable work, and per the pre-prod game-economy audit standard test-depth of this kind is a legitimate but low-priority item. Real gap, over-severe at major; corrected to minor.

description: PayTargetResolver is the only custom ParameterResolver in the component and has no test (no *Resolver*Test.java exists under treasury/src/test). Its suggestions() has real, testable logic: prefix filtering, the 20-item cap (MAX_SUGGESTIONS via subList), the TTL-based government-name cache (GOV_CACHE_TTL_MS), and archived/null-display-name filtering in governmentNames(). None of this is exercised. The charter names ParameterResolvers as pure enough to test directly (suggestions/ambiguity paths) and requires flagging untested custom resolvers. Bukkit.getOnlinePlayers() is mockable via mockStatic, as PayCommandTest already demonstrates.
recommendation: Add a PayTargetResolverTest: mockStatic(Bukkit) for online players, stub AccountService.listGovernmentAccounts(); assert prefix filtering across players+gov names, the 20-suggestion cap, that archived and null-display-name gov accounts are excluded, and that resolve() always returns a PayTarget wrapping the raw token.

description: The account-redirect chain is entirely untested despite being money-adjacent: AccountRedirectMapper (findAllRedirects/findRedirectAccountId/upsertRedirect) is referenced in zero test files; AccountRedirectCache's lazy double-checked-locking warm and authoritative-miss semantics have no test; AccountServiceImpl.findRedirectedAccount (AccountServiceImpl.java:390) is never stubbed or asserted (redirectCache is injected as a bare mock in AccountServiceImplTest but never used); and the redirect branch of LedgerServiceImpl.resolveVaultAccountId (LedgerServiceImpl.java:309-310) — which reroutes legacy government UUIDs like DCGovernment/GovReserve onto their canonical accounts on every Vault deposit/withdraw — is not covered by LedgerServiceTransferIT or LedgerServiceBreadthIT. A broken redirect silently misdirects minted/burned money to the wrong account.
recommendation: Add a mapper IT that upserts and reads redirects against the real MariaDB, an AccountRedirectCache unit test covering warm/hit/authoritative-miss/reload, and a LedgerService IT that seeds an account_redirects row and asserts vaultDeposit/vaultWithdraw for the legacy UUID lands on the redirected account id, not a freshly-minted personal one.

occurrences.snippet:
public java.util.Optional<Integer> findRedirectedAccount(UUID uuid) {
        if (uuid == null) return java.util.Optional.empty();

description: The apiReturnsSkipped and apiReturnsFailed tests exercise the Skipped/Failed branches of BalanceTaxService.collect but assert only the interaction verify(taxApi).collectTax(...) — the same call the happy path makes. They do not assert any observable difference in behaviour between Skipped, Failed, and a successful collect (e.g. no throw propagates, no extra transfer is attempted, or a log/skip counter differs). If the Skipped/Failed handling were deleted and both fell through to the success path, these tests would still pass as long as collectTax is invoked. The branch is entered but its distinct outcome is not pinned.
recommendation: Assert the observable consequence of Skipped/Failed handling: that collect() returns/does not throw and performs no follow-up side effect that a successful collect would (e.g. verify no additional ledger/notifier interaction, or assert the method's return/counter), so deleting the branch fails the test.

description: The message key treasury.general.player-only is defined in messages.properties but is never referenced by any code path (grep for 'player-only' across treasury/src/main returns only this definition, no message.send call). It is a dead i18n entry — a maintenance smell that a startup key-coverage test (see finding 1) would surface bidirectionally. (The placeholder.* keys are framework-consumed and legitimately unreferenced in this module's code.)
recommendation: Either remove the unused treasury.general.player-only key, or wire the player-only guard that was presumably intended to use it; add a bidirectional key-coverage assertion to the startup test so defined-but-unused and used-but-undefined keys both fail CI.
